data.title: Sensient projects mid- to double-digit revenue growth in 2026 as natural color conversion accelerates
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Sensient Technologies Corporation (SXT) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Paul Manning, Chairman, CEO, and President, highlighted, "for the full year of 2025, we delivered 3% local currency revenue growth, 10% local currency adjusted EBITDA growth and 15% local currency adjusted EPS growth." Manning stated that the Color Group led performance, delivering 7.4% local currency revenue growth and 16.9% local currency operating profit growth for the year, with an adjusted EBITDA margin improvement of 160 basis points. He emphasized, "the industry continues to push forward aggressively on this activity in the United States, Canada and parts of Latin America...this conversion to natural colors is the single largest opportunity in the company's history, and we continue our preparations to capture a substantial portion of the commercial opportunities." Manning reiterated the company's investment focus: "We will continue to emphasize investment in research and development, production capacity and a resilient supply chain in order to be ready to support our customers throughout this conversion process."
  • Manning noted, "The Flavors & Extracts Group saw a local currency revenue decline for 2025 of 1.3%, but a 3.4% increase in local currency operating profit... These weather-related impacts further compounded some disruptions to our plant. Together, these issues led to a onetime inventory loss at agricultural ingredients of approximately $3 million in the quarter."
  • Regarding strategic investments, Manning stated, "Our total natural color conversion-related capital expenditures are expected to be between $225 million and $250 million between 2025 and 2028, which will position us to capture the $1 billion sales goal."
  • Tobin Tornehl, VP & CFO, said, "Sensient's revenue was $393.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $376.4 million in last year's fourth quarter. Operating income was $38.2 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $42 million of income in the comparable period last year."

Outlook

  • The company projects, "consolidated annual local currency revenue to grow at a mid- to double-digit rate" for 2026, driven by natural color conversion sales activity. Manning stated, "The Color Group local currency revenue growth should be in the high single-digit to double-digit range. The Flavors & Extracts and Asia Pacific groups should both be in line to deliver mid-single to high single-digit revenue growth in 2026."
  • Tornehl provided 2026 guidance: "We expect our consolidated full year local currency revenue growth to be mid-single digit to double digits... We expect our local currency adjusted EBITDA to also be up mid-single to double digits for the year... We expect our local currency adjusted EPS to be up mid- to high single digits in 2026, with low single-digit growth in the first half and high to double-digit growth in the second half."
  • Manning and Tornehl both indicated that profit leverage will be challenged in the first half of 2026 due to investments, but expect significant acceleration in the second half as natural color conversions ramp up.

Financial Results

  • Tornehl reported, "Sensient's revenue was $393.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $376.4 million in last year's fourth quarter. Operating income was $38.2 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $42 million of income in the comparable period last year."
  • "Excluding the cost of the portfolio optimization plan, adjusted operating income was $44.5 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 compared to $42.9 million in the prior year period."
  • "Interest expense was $7.5 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, up from $6.4 million in the fourth quarter of 2024."
  • "Cash flow from operations was $45 million in the fourth quarter of 2025, up from $21 million in the fourth quarter of 2024. Capital expenditures were $32 million in the fourth quarter of 2025 and $89 million for the full year."
  • Net debt to credit adjusted EBITDA is "2.3x as of December 31, 2025."

Q&A

  • Ghansham Panjabi, Robert W. Baird & Co.: Asked about the $100 million conversion opportunity and 2026 pipeline. Manning replied, "for the 2025, we would have invoiced on the order of about $5 million in Q3 and Q4. So that's just invoiced values. So pretty modest impact to the top line right now...I would anticipate an acceleration in those in Q3 and Q4 as the deadline and as customers self-imposed deadlines to come to get a little bit closer by the end of this year."
  • Panjabi asked about the FDA's impact on conversion cadence and operating leverage. Manning said, "the FDA coming out with some guidance on the labeling...that's not really going to modify the timing...we've made considerable investments throughout 2025. In order to get to the type of $1 billion sales goal...you need more technical people...We've made lots of investments there. We've made investments in our commercial organization. We've made and we've accelerated a lot of our R&D."
  • Lawrence Solow, CJS Securities: Inquired about margin trends and investment normalization. Manning responded, "you'll probably see EBITDA margin down the first half, maybe 100 basis points or so...As you get into the second half, right, you would start to see the EBITDA margin improve versus prior year."
  • Solow also asked about macro supply adaptation. Manning explained, "there's a number of additional players who've entered the crop growing part of this industry. And that's a really positive thing...the more growers there are, the more competition that creates, the more efficiencies that creates."
  • Solow asked about interest expense. Tornehl confirmed, "Overall, our leverage ratio right now is about 2.3. We're guiding to about $36 million in interest, which is up $6 million, as you said, year-over-year, really due to the investments..."
  • Ming Tang, BNP Paribas: Asked about the impact of West Virginia legislation delays. Manning replied, "our customers are very committed to this conversion. And it's not because necessarily of any particular law...it's really fundamentally driven now more and more by this is what the end consumer in the U.S. and Canada wants."
  • Tang sought clarity on Color Group top-line growth. Manning said, "double digits, that sounds really good to me, too...So 10% or better, exactly."
  • Tang asked about Flavors & Extracts and Asia Pacific. Manning said, "for the year, mid-single digit for the Flavor Group, I feel very confident...Asia Pacific...mid-single-digit top line, high single-digit EBITDA for that group as well."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed optimism about the Color Group's growth but remained probing on investment impacts, margin trends, and timing. Their tone was neutral to slightly positive, seeking clarification on guidance and cost structure.
  • Management maintained a confident tone during prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing investment rationale and growth outlook. Manning used phrases like "very, very happy with the Color Group top line" and "very confident and comfortable," but also acknowledged near-term pressure on margins due to investments.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from cautious optimism about conversion timing to a more forward-focused curiosity on profit leverage and ramp-up dynamics. Management’s confidence and long-term focus remained consistent.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter saw continued focus on natural color conversion, but with greater clarity on the timing and magnitude of investments and their impact on margins and profit leverage.
  • Guidance for 2026 is more detailed, projecting mid- to double-digit consolidated revenue growth and significant acceleration in the second half due to natural color conversions. In the previous quarter, guidance called for double-digit growth for EBITDA and EPS for 2025, with less specific detail on 2026 group performance.
  • Both quarters highlighted substantial capital investments, but the current call explicitly quantified $225 million to $250 million in natural color conversion-related capex by 2028.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from regulation-driven conversion timing to the operational and financial impacts of the investment cycle and margin trajectory.
  • Management tone remained confident, continuing to frame near-term margin pressure as necessary for capturing long-term growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Weather-related disruptions led to a onetime $3 million inventory loss in agricultural ingredients for Q4.
  • Tariff-induced supply chain disruptions negatively impacted the Asia Pacific Group.
  • Elevated capital expenditures and higher interest expense are expected to pressure profit leverage and EPS in the near term.
  • Management addressed these risks by emphasizing supply chain investment, expanding grower networks, and maintaining a strong balance sheet to support growth.

Final Takeaway

Sensient Technologies management reinforced its long-term growth strategy centered on the natural color conversion opportunity, projecting mid- to double-digit consolidated revenue growth in 2026 and continued aggressive investment in production capacity, R&D, and supply chain resilience. While margin and profit leverage will be under pressure in the first half due to these investments, management anticipates significant top-line and margin acceleration in the second half, especially within the Color Group. The company remains confident in its ability to capitalize on the multiyear transition from synthetic to natural colors, supported by robust customer commitment and a growing sales pipeline across its business segments.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: U.S. economy overall is ‘weaker than widely anticipated’ - The Leuthold Group’s Jim Paulsen
data.description:
The economy overall is weaker than widely anticipated, according to Jim Paulsen, former chief investment strategist at The Leuthold Group and author of Paulsen Perspectives.

Mark Zandi, chief economist at Moody’s Analytics, also cautioned that current inflation data may be painting an overly optimistic picture.

“The inflation numbers are biased lower because of what happened last October when the government shut down,” Zandi said during a CNBC interview, noting that the BLS couldn’t collect data and effectively assumed no inflation.

When accounting for this anomaly, he said CPI inflation is actually closer to 3% rather than 2%, with prices remaining especially elevated for necessities like housing, childcare, and medical care.

Both analysts expressed deep concern about the stagnant labor market. “There has been effectively no job creation, none since liberation day back in April,” Zandi observed.

Paulsen added that the average unemployment duration has stretched to nearly half a year, calling the situation unacceptable for American workers who face limited prospects when they lose their jobs.

Zandi also pointed to current trade and immigration policies as barriers to economic recovery.

“I think the tariffs are a problem in terms of inflation and in terms of growth,” he argued, suggesting that without broad-based tariffs and heavy-handed immigration policy, inflation would already be back to the Fed’s 2% target.

Paulsen noted a shift in market leadership from “new era” stocks to value and cyclical sectors, driven by easing conditions including money supply growth and a weaker dollar. He said this transition is “big for what’s going on in the stock market as we shift from new era to kind of old era stocks taking the lead.”

Looking ahead, Paulsen urged the Federal Reserve to prioritize employment over strict inflation targets, calling the obsession with 2% inflation “kind of ridiculous.”

With real GDP growth at just 1.8% excluding net exports, he warned the economy is at stall speed.

“I’d rather risk an inflation problem, which I don’t think we have, than the other way around,” Paulsen concluded.

More on the U.S. economy

  • Headline and core CPI inflation rates cool Y/Y in January
  • Miran pushes for more rate cuts as Fed policy is 'tighter than it needs to be'
  • Goldman Sachs’ Solomon: Economic landscape for 2026 is ‘quite good’ with a ‘constructive environment’
  • Markets will be looking for signs of tariff bleed in January CPI
  • Financial stress accelerates to record highs, says the National Foundation for Credit Counseling

data.title: Biggest YTD losses among large-cap software stocks with A profitability grades
data.description:
As AI continues to disrupt the software market, below is a list of the 10 software stocks with A profitability grades and large-market capitalizations, which have the worst price performance YTD. The list focuses on Application Software and Systems Software companies based in the United States.

The list is topped by AppLovin (APP), with a YTD performance of -45.55%. HubSpot (HUBS) and Figma (FIG) follow closely, with YTD losses of -42.95% and -41.08% respectively.

Other notable companies on the list include Intuit (INTU) and Guidewire Software (GWRE), which have experienced significant YTD declines. The list also features established industry leaders such as Salesforce (CRM) and ServiceNow (NOW), both of which have seen losses exceeding 30% year-to-date.

Here is the list:

  1. AppLovin (APP), YTD perf: -45.55%, Quant Rating: Hold 2.93

  2. HubSpot (HUBS), YTD perf: -42.95%, Quant Rating: Strong Sell 1.34

  3. Figma (FIG), YTD perf: -41.08%, Quant Rating: Not Covered

  4. Intuit (INTU), YTD perf: -39.92%, Quant Rating: Hold 2.83

  5. Guidewire Software (GWRE), YTD perf: -37.64%, Quant Rating: Strong Sell 1.30

  6. Tyler Technologies (TYL), YTD perf: -36.77%, Quant Rating: Sell 1.53

  7. Workday (WDAY), YTD perf: -32.94%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.22

  8. ServiceNow (NOW), YTD perf: -32.57%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.16

  9. Rubrik (RBRK), YTD perf: -30.45%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.19

  10. Salesforce (CRM), YTD perf: -30.00%, Quant Rating: Hold 3.38

Software ETFs: (IGPT), (XSW), (AOTS)

data.title: ICON rebounds as Kerrisdale Capital invests after selloff
data.description:
Shares of ICON Public Limited Company (ICLR) climbed ~17% on Friday after Kerrisdale Capital put 20% of its capital into the contract research organization, following a selloff that it said was “far too dramatic.”

Icon (ICLR) lost nearly 40% to hit a new 52-week low on Thursday, recording its worst intraday decline in more than two decades after the Ireland-based firm withdrew its 2025 outlook, citing an investigation into some of its accounting practices.

The company said that its 2023 and 2024 revenue figures may have been overstated by less than 2% due to potential “material weaknesses” in certain internal controls related to its financial reporting.

Icon’s (ICLR) market capitalization dropped to $6.1B after the selloff, which, according to Kerrisdale Capital, is “far too dramatic” for a CRO company of that size and scale.

“The -40% decline on an accounting restatement impacting <2% of revenue was far too dramatic for a leading CRO that gushes $1b of cash flow on now a $6b market cap,” the private investment manager said in an X post, adding, “We put 20% of our capital” into the stock on Thursday.

data.title: Charles Schwab records 18% Y/Y rise in January-end client assets
data.description:
Charles Schwab (SCHW) on Friday said its client assets totaled $12.15 trillion as of January-end, up 18% from January 2025 and 2% compared to December 2025.

Core net new assets at the end of January stood at $27.8B. The flows reflect typical January seasonality.

Transactional sweep cash decreased by $20.4B month-over-month to $433.3M, reflecting expected January seasonality.

A total of 476,000 new brokerage accounts were opened during the month, up 10% year-over-year.

January's daily average trades stood at 9.5M, driven by increased investor engagement in equities. Margin loan balances at the end of the month were $116.3B, an increase of 4% from year-end levels.

data.title: Wall Street moves higher after the latest CPI report
data.description:
Wall Street’s major market averages moved higher on Friday as traders digest the latest round of key retail inflation data.

The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was last higher by +0.4%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) moved up +0.5%, and the tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) advanced by +0.3%. 

From a sector standpoint, nine of the S&P 500’s 11 segments were in positive trading territory, with utilities at the top. Going the other way, communication services has been the weakest performer. 

U.S. Treasury yields shifted lower across the curve. The 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) fell to 4.05%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) was down 4 basis points to 4.69%. The shorter-end 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) fell 5 basis points to 3.40%. 

On the economic front, the January Consumer Price Index came in at +0.2% M/M versus the +0.3% consensus and +0.3% prior readings. At the same time, core CPI (excludes food and energy) arrived at +0.3% M/M versus the +0.3% consensus and +0.2% prior level. 

“Friday's delayed CPI for January was muted and in-line with expectations and it won't increase the likelihood of a rate cut within the next few months largely because of Wednesday's blowout employment numbers, which threw ice cold water on any hopes of a near-term rate cut.  The Fed is always in a tug of war between balancing inflation with employment, but they just can't cut rates right now with the economy having just created a six-figure jobs number,” Skyler Weinand, chief investment officer, of Regan Capital said.

As for stocks that were on the move, shares of Applied Materials (AMAT) climbed 8.8%, and shares of Norwegian Cruise Line (NCLH) came down by 6.7%.

data.title: Anthropic adds former Trump administration official Liddell to board ahead of IPO
data.description:
Anthropic (ANTHRO) has added former Trump administration official Chris Liddell to its board of directors ahead of a potential initial public offering, the company confirmed on Friday.

Liddell, who was the White House Deputy Chief of Staff in the first Trump administration, was also an executive at Microsoft (MSFT) and GM (GM). News of Liddell's appointment was first reported by The Wall Street Journal.

“Chris has spent his career at the intersection of technology, public service, and governance—and has a track record of helping organizations get those things right when the stakes are highest,” said Daniela Amodei, Co-founder and President of Anthropic, in a statement. “As AI’s impact on society grows, that kind of judgement and experience is exactly what we seek on our board.”

“My career has taught me the governance of transformative technologies matters as much as the technologies themselves,” Liddell added. “Anthropic’s approach—building AI that’s both capable and responsible—is critical for our future, and it’s why I’m proud to be joining the company’s Board.”

On Thursday, Anthropic announced it secured $30B in new funding at a $380B valuation. The Series G funding round includes several asset managers and some of the previously announced money from Microsoft and Nvidia (NVDA).

In addition, Anthropic said that the company's revenue run rate is now $14B, and this figure has grown more than 10 times annually over the past three years. Anthropic's revenue run rate surpassed $9B at the end of 2025, according to previous reporting. Included in that is a surge in Claude Code, the company's agentic AI coding tool. Claude Code's revenue run rate is now more than $2.5B and has more than doubled since the start of the year, Anthropic added. Weekly active users for Claude Code have also doubled since Jan. 1, Anthropic explained.

(This story has been updated to reflect Anthropic has confirmed the news.)

data.title: Eversource outlines $26.5B capital plan and targets 5–7% EPS growth through 2028 as regulatory clarity improves
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Eversource Energy (ES) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Joseph Nolan, President, CEO & Chairman, stated that "2025 was another year of strong execution across the organization" and highlighted full year non-GAAP earnings per share of $4.76, along with dividends of $3.01 per share, a 5.2% increase. He emphasized operational performance, capital investments surpassing $4 billion, and significant progress on smart meter installations in Massachusetts, with over 100,000 meters deployed. Nolan also discussed successful collaboration with regulators and policymakers, particularly around affordability initiatives and constructive rate outcomes, and the completion of the onshore substation for the Revolution Wind project.
  • Nolan announced a new 5-year capital investment plan of $26.5 billion, increasing infrastructure investment by $2.3 billion, primarily targeting electric and natural gas distribution. "This new plan increases our necessary infrastructure investment over the next 5 years by $2.3 billion."
  • He confirmed the company’s strategy to continue regulatory engagement, the anticipated sale of Aquarion Water Company, and the pursuit of storm cost recovery in Connecticut, noting, "We are driving forward on several major fronts... executing relentlessly on completing our offshore wind commitments, enhancing storm cost securitization and managing the potential sale of Aquarion."
  • John Moreira, Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer, reported GAAP earnings of $4.56 per share for 2025, including a net loss of $75 million related to the offshore wind sale, and non-GAAP earnings of $4.76 per share. He stated, "Our revised non-GAAP earnings guidance for 2025 was in the range of $4.72 to $4.80." Moreira also detailed segment performance, regulatory decisions, and financing needs, emphasizing the 5-year capital plan and the company’s intent to maintain a cushion over rating agency downgrade thresholds.

Outlook

  • The company projects 2026 earnings per share in the range of $4.80 to $4.95, with earnings growth expected to be more moderate due to timing of regulatory outcomes and headwinds related to Aquarion and storm cost recovery. Moreira explained, "For 2026, we expect earnings growth to be more moderate due primarily to the timing of key regulatory outcomes."
  • Eversource expects a meaningful inflection in earnings growth in 2027 and 2028, supported by improved regulatory outcomes, storm cost recovery, and distribution rate adjustments. The company projects a 5-year long-term EPS growth rate of 5% to 7%, based off 2025 non-GAAP recurring EPS of $4.76 per share, with an expectation to reach the upper half of this range by 2028.
  • The $26.5 billion capital plan reflects increased investments in electric distribution, natural gas distribution, transmission, and technology, excluding Aquarion Water, which would add $1.3 billion if included.

Financial Results

  • Eversource reported non-GAAP earnings of $4.76 per share for 2025, compared to $4.57 per share in 2024. Segment results included electric transmission earnings of $2.09 per share, electric distribution earnings of $1.80 per share, and natural gas distribution earnings of $0.97 per share.
  • The company improved its FFO-to-debt ratio at Moody’s by more than 400 basis points over the last 12 months, with S&P improvement of 300 basis points.
  • The capital investment plan for 2026–2030 totals $26.5 billion, including over $11 billion for electric distribution, nearly $7 billion for natural gas distribution, and over $7 billion for transmission.
  • Regulatory outcomes included PBR rate adjustments, cost recovery settlements, and the approved roll-in of storm and deferred costs in Massachusetts. The company is seeking a preliminary rate request of $88 million in additional revenues for Aquarion in Connecticut.

Q&A

  • Shahriar Pourreza, Wells Fargo: Asked about the impact of the Aquarion sale and storm cost recoveries on funding needs and growth trajectory. Moreira responded that equity needs of $0.8 billion to $1.1 billion are not affected by the Aquarion sale, while alternative financing would be adjusted if the sale is completed. "If Aquarion happens, then the alternative financing solutions will be pulled back."
  • Pourreza also questioned post-close liabilities related to Revolution Wind. Nolan clarified, "The trigger is COD, we deliver just as we did with South Fork. We feel very comfortable with the number that we're carrying now."
  • Carly Davenport, Goldman Sachs: Inquired about minority interest sales and Connecticut AMI investment timing. Moreira described several financing alternatives under consideration. Nolan noted a meeting next week to clarify AMI investment recovery and reiterated that investment would proceed only with a satisfactory recovery mechanism.
  • William Appicelli, UBS: Sought clarity on EPS growth base year and tax benefits from South Fork. Moreira confirmed the upper half of the growth range will be based off 2027 earnings, and that ITC credits remain unused for 2026.
  • Sophie Karp, KeyBanc: Requested timeline details for Revolution Wind’s COD. Nolan replied, "We're targeting the second half of 2026."
  • Paul Patterson, Glenrock Associates: Asked about incremental equity needs if Aquarion is not sold and about the Eversource Gas benefit. Moreira explained the impact on financing needs and that integration cost recoveries are reflected in the parent company.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were focused on funding flexibility, regulatory clarity, and the impact of pending transactions, with a generally neutral to slightly positive tone as questions sought detailed clarification on financing, project timelines, and regulatory processes.
  • Management maintained a confident and disciplined tone in both prepared remarks and Q&A, emphasizing clarity, flexibility, and proactive engagement—"We feel very, very comfortable about our future," Nolan stated. Management was forthcoming with specifics and acknowledged uncertainties while expressing optimism about resolving key overhangs.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analyst sentiment shifted from cautious optimism about regulatory changes and execution to more precise, scenario-based queries. Management’s tone remained confident, but demonstrated increased transparency and readiness to adapt to evolving circumstances.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The current quarter featured a significant increase in capital investment planning, from a $24.2 billion plan to $26.5 billion over five years, and highlighted the achievement of higher non-GAAP earnings per share.
  • Management placed greater emphasis on derisking the business, strengthening the balance sheet, and resolving key open items, while the previous quarter focused on regulatory progress and operational achievements.
  • Analysts’ questions shifted from broad regulatory and operational concerns to direct inquiries about funding strategies, project timelines, and the incremental impact of pending outcomes like the Aquarion sale and storm cost recovery.
  • Management’s confidence remained steady, but the current quarter reflected more detail on financing options, regulatory proceedings, and execution milestones.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited uncertainties regarding the sale of Aquarion Water and storm cost recoveries in Connecticut, emphasizing ongoing regulatory engagement and contingency planning.
  • The potential for higher financing needs if the Aquarion sale is not completed was acknowledged, with a focus on alternative funding solutions and maintaining financial discipline.
  • Analysts raised concerns about project timelines, regulatory approval processes, and the structure of potential minority interest sales. Management reiterated that investment decisions—particularly for Connecticut AMI—would be made only with approved recovery mechanisms.

Final Takeaway

Eversource management reiterated their focus on delivering operational excellence, advancing critical infrastructure investments, and maintaining financial flexibility. The company expects a transitional year in 2026 with moderate earnings growth, but projects a clear path to the upper half of its 5% to 7% long-term EPS growth range by 2028, underpinned by regulatory clarity, disciplined execution, and a strengthened balance sheet. Key milestones, including the resolution of Aquarion and storm cost recovery, are expected to provide further upside in the outer years of the forecast period.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
